description:
You are acting as a release-manager who is getting today’s deployment paperwork in order.

  1.  Inside /home/user/releases two data sources are already present:  
      • /home/user/releases/environments.csv – a small CSV whose first column is the environment name.  
      • /home/user/releases/versions.txt   – a space-separated text file whose second field is the version tag for each environment.  
      (Concrete file layouts are given in the next paragraph.)

  2.  Your job is to create a brand-new, tab-separated plan called
      /home/user/releases/deployment_plan.tsv with exactly **four** lines:

         Environment<TAB>Version<TAB>Ready
         prod<TAB>v2.3.1<TAB>no
         staging<TAB>v2.3.1-rc<TAB>no
         qa<TAB>v2.3.0<TAB>no

      – The first line is a header.  
      – Lines must be separated by a single UNIX newline (`\n`).  
      – Each column must be separated by one ASCII TAB character (0x09).  
      – No extra spaces, no carriage-returns, no trailing blank lines.

      Achieve this with the standard text-processing utilities “cut” and “paste”; no scripting languages are necessary.  
      (One reasonable approach is: cut the environment names from the first file, cut the version column from the second, paste them together, and then prepend the header row and append the literal TAB-separated “no” status column.)

  3.  For repeatability we also need a short log entry.  
      Create (or append to if it already exists) /home/user/releases/logs/deployment_actions.log.  
      Add exactly one new line formatted like this (today’s date example shown – substitute the date you actually run the command):

         2025-04-11 CREATED deployment_plan.tsv

      Date must be in ISO-8601 `YYYY-MM-DD` format; use today’s date inside the running container.  
      Separate the three fields with single spaces and terminate the line with `\n`.

  4.  When you finish, confirm that:  
      • /home/user/releases/deployment_plan.tsv exists and its 4 lines match the format shown above byte-for-byte (aside from today’s date in the log).  
      • /home/user/releases/logs/deployment_actions.log contains the new log line as the last line in the file.

  Remember: do not hard-code the TSV file; build it using “cut” and “paste”.  Keep the solution to a succinct set of 3-4 shell commands.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing source files -->
  <preexisting>
    <directory path="/home/user/releases"/>
    <file path="/home/user/releases/environments.csv"><![CDATA[
env,owner
prod,alice
staging,bob
qa,charlie
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/releases/versions.txt"><![CDATA[
prod v2.3.1
staging v2.3.1-rc
qa v2.3.0
]]></file>

    <directory path="/home/user/releases/logs"/>
  </preexisting>

  <!-- Expected artefacts after the agent finishes -->
  <expected>
    <file path="/home/user/releases/deployment_plan.tsv"><![CDATA[
Environment	Version	Ready
prod	v2.3.1	no
staging	v2.3.1-rc	no
qa	v2.3.0	no
]]></file>

    <!-- The log must contain the new line as the LAST line.  We use DATE_PLACEHOLDER here;
         the harness replaces it by the current date before checksum comparison. -->
    <file path="/home/user/releases/logs/deployment_actions.log" contains_regex="^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2} CREATED deployment_plan\\.tsv$"/>
  </expected>
name: task_000000_cc48ff40
